tweets.text:
Levered ETFs have become one of the most powerful forces in US equity markets:

Daily rebalancing activity in US equity leveraged ETFs is up to a record $50 billion.

Leveraged ETFs must rebalance daily to stay at their target leverage, adding more exposure after markets rise and reducing it after markets fall.

This figure has more than QUADRUPLED since the start of 2026.

Rebalancing activity now accounts for a record 1.60% of S&P 500 futures volume, exceeding the 2020-2024 peaks by 200%.

To put it simply, leveraged ETFs have grown large enough that their daily rebalancing can now amplify market moves in both directions.

Leveraged ETFs are amplifying market volatility like never before.

The jobs report today looked recessionary. Household employment -500K. Non-farm payroll +57K, about half of consensus. Labor force participation fell again.

But we don't think this is a recession signal. We think it's a measurement problem. Our take on today's In The Know with @CathieDWood. https://t.co/8KKU8BOt2W

tweets.text:
When you deposit into a vault, you're exposed to more than the vault itself: oracles, bridges, collateral, the sequencer, holders of vault roles.

Introducing risk scenario planning on DefiLlama.

* Maps every dependency that touches your deposit
* Models your max loss from each failure point
* Live now across 20 Morpho vaults

tweets.text:
🇨🇳 #China Tech Stock Selloff Temporary, AI Trend Intact - China Securities Journal

➡ The sharp pullback in the A-share market led by tech on Thursday is a technical reset from overcrowded trades in the first half, while AI’s long-term structural investment value remains intact, the China Securities Journal reported, citing analysts.

➡ The market correction stems from stretched valuations and fragile positioning.

➡ Capital inflows into A-shares will continue, with improving earnings and easing external risks set to support broader market gains, it said, citing research from Guosen Securities.

➡ Central China Securities analyst Xu Zhi recommended a barbell portfolio to mitigate market volatility: high-growth plays including AI computing hardware and smart manufacturing, paired with defensive high-dividend banks and utilities.

*Link (Chinese): https://t.co/Ac9lNun4ws
tweets.full_text:
🇨🇳 #China Tech Stock Selloff Temporary, AI Trend Intact - China Securities Journal

➡ The sharp pullback in the A-share market led by tech on Thursday is a technical reset from overcrowded trades in the first half, while AI’s long-term structural investment value remains intact, the China Securities Journal reported, citing analysts.

➡ The market correction stems from stretched valuations and fragile positioning.

➡ Capital inflows into A-shares will continue, with improving earnings and easing external risks set to support broader market gains, it said, citing research from Guosen Securities.

➡ Central China Securities analyst Xu Zhi recommended a barbell portfolio to mitigate market volatility: high-growth plays including AI computing hardware and smart manufacturing, paired with defensive high-dividend banks and utilities.

*Link (Chinese): https://t.co/Ac9lNun4ws

tweets.text:
What a quarter.

The S&P 500 rose 14.9% from the end of March through June.  

After the prior 20 strong second quarters since 1950, the next 9 months were positive every time. 
20 for 20.

Read full analysis: https://t.co/Gp9hO1kSPw https://t.co/jWw4izD077

tweets.text:
The US once added $3,000 to $4,000 of real housing per household each year. 

Today it's under $2,000.

After inflation and depreciation, the country adds less housing per household than it did 60 years ago. 

2/ https://t.co/xV6apry5cD

thread_tweets.text:
"Housing is expensive because of zoning laws and interest rates." 

That answer isn't wrong. It's just incomplete. 

The bigger story: America stopped building physical structures, and it no longer has the money for the investment it does make. 

Here's the data: 

1/

The bigger story: America stopped building physical structures, and it no longer has the money for the investment it does make.

1/

thread_tweets.text:
So of the investment we do make, more and more is funded by the foreign sector. 

But there's an even bigger shift going on.

The mix or the blend of what we actually invest in.

4/
thread_tweets.full_text:
So of the investment we do make, more and more is funded by the foreign sector. 

But there's an even bigger shift going on.

The mix or the blend of what we actually invest in.

4/

thread_tweets.text:
We have to start with who funds our investment. 

Decades ago, America had high savings, and we funded our own investments. 

Today, net national saving is near 0%, so we fund almost all our investment with savings from abroad.

3/ https://t.co/jqrFZSRRVu
thread_tweets.full_text:
We have to start with who funds our investment. 

Decades ago, America had high savings, and we funded our own investments. 

Today, net national saving is near 0%, so we fund almost all our investment with savings from abroad.

3/ https://t.co/jqrFZSRRVu

thread_tweets.text:
Housing didn't just shrink as an investment. 

It got more expensive to build at the same time.

Real net residential investment fell from about 5% of GDP in the 1970s to roughly 1% today, while the cost to build has run about 4% a year for decades, far above CPI.

6/ https://t.co/0ctW0xxIxt
thread_tweets.full_text:
Housing didn't just shrink as an investment. 

It got more expensive to build at the same time.

Real net residential investment fell from about 5% of GDP in the 1970s to roughly 1% today, while the cost to build has run about 4% a year for decades, far above CPI.

6/ https://t.co/0ctW0xxIxt

thread_tweets.text:
Split investment in two: 

*Structures (homes, factories, power grids) 

*Equipment plus IP (computer equipment, software).

In the 1960s, structures ran 7 to 9% of GDP, and equipment and IP under 1%. 

Today they're about 2.1% each. 

5/ https://t.co/frOaV7vtPe
thread_tweets.full_text:
Split investment in two: 

*Structures (homes, factories, power grids) 

*Equipment plus IP (computer equipment, software).

In the 1960s, structures ran 7 to 9% of GDP, and equipment and IP under 1%. 

Today they're about 2.1% each. 

5/ https://t.co/frOaV7vtPe

thread_tweets.text:
The digital economy (software, chips) keeps getting cheaper, and those gains flow to a small group of owners.

The physical economy everyone else lives on keeps getting scarcer and more expensive.

8/
thread_tweets.full_text:
The digital economy (software, chips) keeps getting cheaper, and those gains flow to a small group of owners.

The physical economy everyone else lives on keeps getting scarcer and more expensive.

8/

thread_tweets.text:
Some may call the shift from structures to IP progress for an advanced economy. 

This is certainly true. 

But it splits the country in two (or into a K).

7/
thread_tweets.full_text:
Some may call the shift from structures to IP progress for an advanced economy. 

This is certainly true. 

But it splits the country in two (or into a K).

7/

thread_tweets.text:
We've held real net investment near 5% of GDP only because foreign money fills the gap. 

With domestic savings near zero, if those foreign inflows slow, the gap has to close through higher rates, less investment, or squeezed spending.

9/ https://t.co/M19YWv5eS9

tweets.full_text:
The US once added $3,000 to $4,000 of real housing per household each year. 

Today it's under $2,000.

After inflation and depreciation, the country adds less housing per household than it did 60 years ago. 

2/ https://t.co/xV6apry5cD

"Housing is expensive because of zoning laws and interest rates." 

That answer isn't wrong. It's just incomplete. 

The bigger story: America stopped building physical structures, and it no longer has the money for the investment it does make. 

Here's the data: 

1/

So of the investment we do make, more and more is funded by the foreign sector. 

But there's an even bigger shift going on.

The mix or the blend of what we actually invest in.

4/

We have to start with who funds our investment. 

Decades ago, America had high savings, and we funded our own investments. 

Today, net national saving is near 0%, so we fund almost all our investment with savings from abroad.

3/ https://t.co/jqrFZSRRVu

Housing didn't just shrink as an investment. 

It got more expensive to build at the same time.

Real net residential investment fell from about 5% of GDP in the 1970s to roughly 1% today, while the cost to build has run about 4% a year for decades, far above CPI.

6/ https://t.co/0ctW0xxIxt

Split investment in two: 

*Structures (homes, factories, power grids) 

*Equipment plus IP (computer equipment, software).

In the 1960s, structures ran 7 to 9% of GDP, and equipment and IP under 1%. 

Today they're about 2.1% each. 

5/ https://t.co/frOaV7vtPe

The digital economy (software, chips) keeps getting cheaper, and those gains flow to a small group of owners.

The physical economy everyone else lives on keeps getting scarcer and more expensive.

8/

Some may call the shift from structures to IP progress for an advanced economy. 

This is certainly true. 

But it splits the country in two (or into a K).

7/

We've held real net investment near 5% of GDP only because foreign money fills the gap. 

With domestic savings near zero, if those foreign inflows slow, the gap has to close through higher rates, less investment, or squeezed spending.

9/ https://t.co/M19YWv5eS9

I lay out this entire thesis in greater detail, with more charts, in the full article.

You can read it here: https://t.co/ljhPobGaBo

10/10
